refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion tied to acceptance, not a functional requirement. This separation allows DPs to address enablement independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and site conditions, the NGHE solution permits completion of load→travel→dump without artificial time constraints imposed by the system itself. Measure p95 cycle time against CT_target as an acceptance criterion; if p95 exceeds target, root-cause analysis determines whether the FR was not met (system imposed delays) or whether operational/environmental factors outside the FR scope are responsible.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable load acquisition within the cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable travel (loaded and unloaded) within the cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable material dump within the cycle time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize idle/transition time between load, travel, and dump phases.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose cycle into discrete, sequenced phases (load, travel, dump) with phase-gating logic that enforces strict ordering and allows independent optimization of each phase duration.

DP2: Implement a centralized cycle-time budget allocation mechanism that distributes CT_target across phases, with real-time monitoring and adaptive phase-duration adjustment to maintain p95 compliance.

DP3: Design a predictive cycle-time model that accounts for site conditions (terrain, load mass, distance) and pre-computes optimal phase parameters before cycle execution, with fallback to nominal timing.

DP4: Establish a closed-loop feedback control system that measures actual phase durations against budgeted targets and applies corrective actions (speed modulation, path optimization) within each phase.

DP5: Decouple cycle-time performance from external variability (site conditions, load variance) by introducing a buffering or scheduling layer that absorbs variance and guarantees deterministic phase transitions.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP2 + DP4 (integrated): Implement a centralized cycle-time budget allocation mechanism that distributes CT_target across load, travel, and dump phases with explicit phase-duration targets. Pair this with closed-loop feedback control that monitors actual phase durations in real-time, detects deviations from budget, and applies corrective actions (e.g., speed adjustment, path re-optimization) to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target under site variability. This design separates budget planning (DP2) from execution control (DP4), enabling independent tuning of allocation strategy and feedback responsiveness.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between phase-duration targets and site-condition models: if terrain/load prediction is inaccurate, budget allocation fails. Mitigation: use conservative (pessimistic) initial budgets and adaptive re-allocation.

Feedback control loop responsiveness vs. phase duration: if corrective actions (e.g., speed change) have long latency relative to phase duration, control may be ineffective. Mitigation: ensure actuation bandwidth is ≥ 3× fastest phase duration.

Cross-phase dependencies: dump phase may be blocked if travel phase overruns; load phase may be blocked if dump phase is slow. Mitigation: design phases as independent state machines with explicit handoff protocols.
Measurement coupling: if cycle-time sensors are unreliable or have high latency, feedback control becomes unstable. Mitigation: specify sensor accuracy and latency requirements explicitly.
coupling_check.notes: DP2+DP4 avoids monolithic cycle design and supports independent optimization of allocation vs. control. However, success depends critically on accurate site-condition modeling and low-latency feedback. DP1 (phase decomposition) is a prerequisite; DP3 (predictive modeling) is a supporting enabler. Coupling risks are significant and must be resolved in DP realization before proceeding.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Site conditions (terrain, load mass, distance) are measurable or predictable with sufficient accuracy to inform initial budget allocation. RISK: If site variability is high and unpredictable, budget allocation will be conservative, reducing throughput. MITIGATION: Implement adaptive re-allocation based on first few cycles.

ASSUMPTION: Actuation system (drive motors, hydraulics, steering) can respond to corrective commands (speed, path changes) with latency ≤ 10% of phase duration. RISK: If latency is high, feedback control cannot correct deviations within a single phase. MITIGATION: Specify actuation bandwidth in DP realization; consider predictive control if latency is unavoidable.

ASSUMPTION: Cycle-time measurement (via sensors, GPS, or odometry) is accurate to ±5% of CT_target. RISK: If measurement noise is high, feedback control will oscillate or diverge. MITIGATION: Implement low-pass filtering and deadband logic in feedback loop.

ASSUMPTION: Load, travel, and dump phases can be executed sequentially without blocking or resource contention. RISK: If phases share actuators or hydraulic circuits, phase transitions may be delayed. MITIGATION: Validate phase independence in DP realization; design hydraulic/electrical architecture to support concurrent phase preparation.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined CT_target is achievable under stated duty cycle and site conditions. RISK: If CT_target is unrealistic (e.g., requires speeds exceeding safety limits), no DP can satisfy FR. MITIGATION: Validate CT_target feasibility early; document assumptions about load mass, distance, and terrain.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan does not impose hard constraints that conflict with cycle-time optimization (e.g., mandatory speed limits, dwell times). RISK: If safety constraints are binding, CT_target may be unachievable. MITIGATION: Coordinate with safety team; ensure DP respects all safety policies.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) the acceptable range of site conditions (terrain type, load mass, distance) under which p95 ≤ CT_target must hold; (2) the measurement method and accuracy for cycle-time validation; (3) any hard constraints from the worksite safety plan that affect phase execution (e.g., speed limits, dwell times, operator override requirements). Additionally, clarify whether CT_target is a hard deadline or a statistical target, and whether the system must adapt to site variability or assume fixed conditions. This will enable DP realization to specify actuation bandwidth, feedback control tuning, and budget allocation strategy with confidence.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized cycle-time budget allocation engine and closed-loop feedback control system that owns budget planning logic, phase-duration targets, real-time deviation detection, and corrective action orchestration across load, travel, and dump phases.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives real-time phase duration observations, actual speeds, path metrics, and site variability signals from Vehicle to enable closed-loop feedback control and deviation detection.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends corrective action directives (speed adjustments, path re-optimization requests, phase-duration target updates) to Vehicle and Teleoperation to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints that bound corrective actions; sends alerts if budget allocation or feedback control decisions conflict with safety thresholds.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for mission-level optimization logic, budget planning, and closed-loop control orchestration. Budget allocation (DP2) and feedback control (DP4) are both strategic/algorithmic decisions that belong in the planning/optimization layer. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry (input) and Tasking (output) cross boundary; SafetyAlert integration is localized. The separation of budget planning from execution control is cleanly realized within NGHE's decision-making scope.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical/execution platform, not a planning/control logic owner. Placing budget allocation and feedback control logic in Vehicle would scatter strategic decision-making across the platform, creating tight coupling between vehicle firmware and mission optimization. Corrective actions (speed, path) would need to diffuse back to NGHE for coordination. Violates separation of concerns.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop interface layer, not an autonomous control engine. Budget allocation and closed-loop feedback control are algorithmic/autonomous functions that should not reside in a teleoperation module. High diffusion: would require constant bidirectional communication with NGHE for planning and with Vehicle for execution. Violates module cohesion.

Poor fit. Safety Services is a constraint/validation layer, not a budget allocation or feedback control engine. While Safety Services must validate that corrective actions respect safety bounds, it should not own the budget planning or control logic itself. High diffusion: would require Safety Services to coordinate with NGHE for planning and Vehicle for execution. Violates single responsibility principle.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The integrated DP (budget allocation + closed-loop feedback control) is a mission-level optimization and autonomous control function that belongs in the NGHE Solution layer. NGHE already owns strategic decision-making, task planning, and coordination logic. Placing both budget planning (DP2) and feedback control (DP4) in NGHE preserves the intended separation of concerns: NGHE handles planning and control logic; Vehicle handles execution; Teleoperation handles human oversight; Safety Services handles constraint validation. The boundary is clean, with well-defined interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert bidirectional).
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic 'god module' without internal decomposition, budget allocation and feedback control logic may become entangled with other NGHE functions (e.g., task sequencing, resource allocation). Mitigation: Internally decompose NGHE into sub-modules (e.g., CycleTimeBudgetPlanner, FeedbackControlEngine) with clear interfaces.

Risk: Real-time feedback control (DP4) may require sub-millisecond latency that NGHE cannot guarantee if it is remote or computationally overloaded. Mitigation: Validate that NGHE's deployment architecture (edge vs. cloud) and computational capacity support the required control loop frequency. If latency is critical, consider a hybrid approach where Vehicle hosts a lightweight feedback controller that NGHE supervises.

Risk: Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck if Vehicle sends high-frequency phase-duration and speed data. Mitigation: Define a clear telemetry schema and sampling rate; use filtering/aggregation to reduce data volume.

Risk: SafetyAlert bidirectional coupling may create circular dependencies if Safety Services also monitors NGHE's corrective actions. Mitigation: Establish clear precedence: Safety Services validates NGHE's actions; NGHE respects Safety Services' constraints but does not override them.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP2 + DP4 (integrated) directly and sufficiently addresses FR. The centralized cycle-time budget allocation mechanism (DP2) logically decomposes CT_target into phase-duration targets, and closed-loop feedback control (DP4) ensures real-time deviation detection and corrective action to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target. The separation of budget planning from execution control is sound and enables independent tuning. No logical gaps identified between functional requirement and design parameters.

diffusion_risks: NGHE Solution containment score of 0.92 is strong, but internal decomposition risk exists: budget allocation logic (DP2) and feedback control logic (DP4) must be internally modularized within NGHE (e.g., CycleTimeBudgetPlanner and FeedbackControlEngine sub-modules) to prevent entanglement with other NGHE functions (task sequencing, resource allocation). Without internal boundaries, DP2 and DP4 may diffuse across NGHE's codebase.

Real-time feedback control (DP4) latency risk: if NGHE is deployed remotely or is computationally constrained, sub-millisecond control loop frequency required for phase-duration correction may not be achievable. This could force diffusion of lightweight feedback logic to Vehicle firmware, violating the intended DP<->IM mapping. Mitigation: validate NGHE deployment architecture and latency SLA before gate approval.

Telemetry interface data volume risk: high-frequency phase-duration, speed, and path metrics from Vehicle to NGHE may saturate the Telemetry channel. Unmitigated, this could force filtering/aggregation logic to diffuse to Vehicle, blurring the boundary. Mitigation: define explicit telemetry schema and sampling rate constraints.

SafetyAlert bidirectional coupling risk: if Safety Services also monitors NGHE's corrective actions in real-time, circular dependencies may emerge (NGHE requests action → Safety validates → NGHE adjusts → Safety re-validates). This can create diffusion of validation logic across both modules. Mitigation: establish clear precedence and one-way constraint flow: Safety Services validates; NGHE respects but does not override.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary for the integrated DP. Budget allocation and closed-loop feedback control are mission-level optimization and autonomous control functions that logically belong in the planning/coordination layer, not in Vehicle (execution platform), Teleoperation (human interface), or Safety Services (constraint validation). The IM boundary is clean with well-defined external interfaces: Telemetry (in), Tasking (out), SafetyAlert (bidirectional). However, containment quality depends critically on: (1) internal decomposition of NGHE to isolate DP2 and DP4 logic, (2) validation of NGHE's latency and computational capacity to support real-time feedback control, and (3) clear interface contracts (telemetry schema, sampling rate, SafetyAlert precedence) to prevent diffusion across boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is measurable and directly traceable to DP4 (closed-loop feedback control). NGHE Solution's ownership of budget allocation (DP2) and feedback control (DP4) enables end-to-end verification: (1) validate budget allocation logic against phase-duration targets in simulation, (2) validate feedback control responsiveness in field trials under site variability, (3) measure p95 cycle time against CT_target. Telemetry interface provides observability; Tasking interface enables corrective action injection. Verification is feasible provided NGHE's internal decomposition and latency SLA are validated.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR is allocated to DP2 + DP4 (integrated), which is owned by NGHE Solution. The allocation is unambiguous: NGHE owns the mission-level optimization and control logic; Vehicle owns execution; Teleoperation owns human oversight; Safety Services owns constraint validation. However, allocation clarity depends on explicit documentation of: (1) NGHE's internal sub-module boundaries (CycleTimeBudgetPlanner, FeedbackControlEngine), (2) latency and computational SLA for feedback control, (3) telemetry schema and sampling rate, (4) SafetyAlert precedence and constraint flow. Without these details, allocation clarity may degrade during implementation.

recommended_revisions: Decompose NGHE Solution internally into at least two sub-modules: (1) CycleTimeBudgetPlanner (owns DP2 logic: budget allocation, phase-duration target calculation, allocation strategy tuning), (2) FeedbackControlEngine (owns DP4 logic: real-time deviation detection, corrective action orchestration, control loop frequency tuning). Define clear interfaces between these sub-modules and with external systems (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert).

Validate NGHE's deployment architecture (edge vs. cloud), computational capacity, and network latency to confirm that feedback control loop frequency (DP4) can achieve required responsiveness for phase-duration correction under site variability. If latency SLA cannot be met, document a hybrid approach where Vehicle hosts a lightweight feedback controller supervised by NGHE.

Define explicit Telemetry interface contract: specify phase-duration observation schema, sampling rate (e.g., per-phase or per-cycle), data aggregation/filtering rules, and maximum data volume. Ensure Vehicle does not diffuse filtering logic; NGHE owns filtering/aggregation decisions.

Establish SafetyAlert precedence and constraint flow: document that Safety Services validates NGHE's corrective actions against safety thresholds, and NGHE respects Safety Services' constraints without override. Define escalation path if budget allocation or feedback control conflicts with safety bounds (e.g., alert to Teleoperation, pause corrective actions).

Add traceability matrix linking FR → DP2 (budget allocation) → CycleTimeBudgetPlanner (IM sub-module) and FR → DP4 (feedback control) → FeedbackControlEngine (IM sub-module) to ensure no diffusion of logic across module boundaries during implementation.

Document acceptance test plan for p95 ≤ CT_target: specify test scenarios (nominal, site variability, edge cases), measurement methodology (phase-duration sampling, p95 calculation), and success criteria. Ensure test plan validates both DP2 (budget allocation correctness) and DP4 (feedback control responsiveness).

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence in the logical mapping and ownership boundary. However, the DP-IM gate identifies critical diffusion risks and containment dependencies that cannot be resolved at the current level of abstraction. The recommended revisions explicitly call for internal decomposition of NGHE Solution into sub-modules (CycleTimeBudgetPlanner, FeedbackControlEngine) with defined interfaces, latency/computational SLA validation, and explicit interface contracts (Telemetry schema, SafetyAlert precedence). These are not minor implementation details—they are architectural decisions that affect the feasibility and safety of the design. Proceeding to the next decomposition level is necessary to: (1) make internal NGHE module boundaries explicit and analyzable, (2) validate latency and computational capacity constraints that could force diffusion to Vehicle firmware, (3) define interface contracts to prevent logic diffusion across system boundaries. High safety criticality reinforces the need for explicit sub-level decomposition to ensure control loop responsiveness and constraint validation are architecturally sound before implementation.
next_level_sub_frs: FR2.1: Allocate cycle-time budget to mission phases based on duty cycle, site conditions, and optimization strategy, such that phase-duration targets are feasible and collectively achieve CT_target.

FR2.2: Detect real-time deviations in phase duration and speed against allocated targets, and orchestrate corrective actions (task resequencing, resource reallocation, speed adjustment) to maintain p95 ≤ CT_target under site variability.
FR2.3: Validate that corrective actions proposed by cycle-time feedback control do not violate safety constraints (speed limits, collision avoidance, emergency stop precedence) before execution.

FR2.4: Provide observability of phase-duration, speed, and path metrics to NGHE feedback control via Telemetry interface with defined schema, sampling rate, and data volume constraints to enable real-time deviation detection without saturating communication channels.

revise.why: The current DP2 + DP4 formulation is logically sound but insufficiently detailed to guide internal NGHE decomposition and interface design. Revise DPs to explicitly address: (1) budget allocation strategy (DP2 sub-logic: phase-duration target calculation, allocation algorithm, tuning parameters), (2) feedback control mechanism (DP4 sub-logic: deviation detection threshold, corrective action priority, control loop frequency, latency SLA), (3) safety constraint validation (new DP: precedence rules, escalation logic), (4) telemetry interface contract (new DP: schema definition, sampling rate, aggregation rules). This revision will make the DP layer sufficiently detailed to map cleanly to NGHE's internal sub-modules and external interfaces, reducing diffusion risk during implementation.

assumptions: NGHE Solution has sufficient computational capacity and network latency (< 100 ms round-trip) to support real-time feedback control loop frequency required for phase-duration correction. If this assumption is violated, feedback control logic may need to diffuse to Vehicle firmware.

Telemetry interface can be designed with explicit schema and sampling rate constraints (e.g., per-phase phase-duration observations, aggregated speed metrics) such that data volume does not exceed communication channel capacity. If this assumption is violated, filtering/aggregation logic may diffuse to Vehicle.

Safety Services operates with clear precedence: Safety validates NGHE's corrective actions; NGHE respects Safety constraints without override. Circular dependencies and validation logic diffusion are avoided by establishing one-way constraint flow.

NGHE's internal decomposition into CycleTimeBudgetPlanner and FeedbackControlEngine sub-modules is feasible and does not introduce unintended coupling with other NGHE functions (task sequencing, resource allocation). This assumption requires architectural review during detailed design.

Phase-duration targets calculated by DP2 (budget allocation) are achievable under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions. If targets are infeasible, feedback control (DP4) cannot correct deviations, and FR cannot be satisfied.
open_risks: NGHE latency and computational capacity: If NGHE is deployed remotely (cloud) or is computationally constrained, sub-millisecond feedback control loop frequency may not be achievable. This could force diffusion of lightweight feedback logic to Vehicle firmware, violating the intended DP<->IM mapping and creating a hybrid control architecture that is harder to verify and maintain. Mitigation: Validate NGHE deployment architecture and latency SLA before detailed design; if latency SLA cannot be met, document hybrid approach with clear ownership boundaries.

Telemetry data volume saturation: High-frequency phase-duration, speed, and path metrics from Vehicle to NGHE may exceed communication channel capacity. Unmitigated, this could force filtering/aggregation logic to diffuse to Vehicle, blurring the boundary between Vehicle (execution) and NGHE (optimization). Mitigation: Define explicit telemetry schema, sampling rate (e.g., per-phase aggregation), and maximum data volume; validate against communication channel capacity.

SafetyAlert bidirectional coupling: If Safety Services monitors NGHE's corrective actions in real-time and NGHE adjusts actions based on Safety feedback, circular dependencies may emerge. This can create diffusion of validation logic across both modules and introduce control instability. Mitigation: Establish clear precedence: Safety Services validates; NGHE respects but does not override. Define escalation path if conflict occurs (e.g., alert to Teleoperation, pause corrective actions).

Internal NGHE decomposition entanglement: Budget allocation logic (DP2) and feedback control logic (DP4) must be internally modularized within NGHE to prevent entanglement with task sequencing and resource allocation functions. Without explicit internal boundaries, DP2 and DP4 logic may diffuse across NGHE's codebase, making verification and maintenance difficult. Mitigation: Define CycleTimeBudgetPlanner and FeedbackControlEngine as distinct sub-modules with clear interfaces; enforce module boundaries during implementation.

Phase-duration target feasibility: If budget allocation (DP2) produces phase-duration targets that are infeasible under site variability, feedback control (DP4) cannot correct deviations, and FR cannot be satisfied. This risk is high if site conditions are poorly characterized or duty cycle assumptions are violated. Mitigation: Validate phase-duration targets in simulation and field trials; establish feedback loop to adjust allocation strategy if targets are consistently infeasible.
evidence_needed: NGHE deployment architecture specification: document whether NGHE is deployed on-vehicle (edge), at a remote facility (cloud), or hybrid; specify network latency SLA, computational capacity (CPU, memory, I/O), and feedback control loop frequency requirement. Validate that latency and capacity constraints can be met.

Telemetry interface contract: define phase-duration observation schema (e.g., per-phase duration, speed profile, path metrics), sampling rate (e.g., per-phase or per-cycle), data aggregation/filtering rules, and maximum data volume. Validate against Vehicle-to-NGHE communication channel capacity.

SafetyAlert precedence and constraint flow documentation: specify that Safety Services validates NGHE's corrective actions against safety thresholds (speed limits, collision avoidance, emergency stop), and NGHE respects Safety constraints without override. Define escalation path if budget allocation or feedback control conflicts with safety bounds.

NGHE internal sub-module design: document CycleTimeBudgetPlanner and FeedbackControlEngine as distinct sub-modules with defined responsibilities, interfaces, and dependencies. Specify budget allocation algorithm, feedback control loop frequency, deviation detection threshold, and corrective action priority rules.

Phase-duration target feasibility analysis: validate in simulation that phase-duration targets calculated by DP2 are achievable under the agreed duty cycle and site conditions (e.g., terrain variability, weather, traffic). Identify edge cases where targets may be infeasible and document mitigation strategy.

Acceptance test plan for p95 ≤ CT_target: specify test scenarios (nominal, site variability, edge cases, safety constraint violations), measurement methodology (phase-duration sampling, p95 calculation), and success criteria. Ensure test plan validates both DP2 (budget allocation correctness) and DP4 (feedback control responsiveness) under realistic conditions.

Traceability matrix: link FR → DP2 (budget allocation) → CycleTimeBudgetPlanner (IM sub-module) and FR → DP4 (feedback control) → FeedbackControlEngine (IM sub-module) to ensure no diffusion of logic across module boundaries during implementation.
